skill_name: SQL Injection and DB Query Correctness Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Database access layer, Raw SQL construction in src/db/users.ts, User-controlled input handling for lookup queries
risk_profile: SQL injection through unsafe interpolation
Missing parameter binding for user-controlled values
Escaping or string formatting mistaken for parameterization
Query construction that changes semantics for special characters
Sensitive data exposure through overly broad query behavior
Unhandled promise rejections
Swallowed errors leading to silent failures
context_plan: Focus on Database access layer
Focus on Raw SQL construction in src/db/users.ts
Focus on User-controlled input handling for lookup queries
Focus on DB client API usage consistency
Focus on Behavioral regression in user lookup
checklist: Does any SQL string interpolate request, route, form, or function parameters directly?
Verify getUserByName uses parameter binding instead of template literal interpolation.
Are placeholders paired with complete argument arrays or query-builder bindings?
Confirm the db.query signature matches expected repository usage for parameterized queries.
Does the fix avoid manual escaping as the primary defense?
Could wildcard, quote, comment, or boolean payloads alter query semantics?
Check that names containing quotes or SQL comment syntax are treated as data, not executable SQL.
Ensure normal user-name lookup still returns expected records after restoring parameterization.
Check whether special-character input changes lookup behavior or causes syntax errors.
Confirm the database mock or tests assert bound parameters are passed to db.query.
Are tests proposed for both malicious input and normal lookup behavior?

skill_name: User Cache Correctness, Security & Consistency Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Cache invalidation and write-through behavior on user mutation paths, Repository-grounded verification of all user cache readers and writers, Authorization/security impact of stale cached user state
risk_profile: Stale reads after mutation
Cache/database inconsistency
Incorrect behavior in auth/profile/account-state flows due to outdated cached state
Partial failure between DB and cache operations
TTL-driven hidden consistency regressions
Performance regressions from unnecessary cache churn
context_plan: Focus on Cache invalidation and write-through behavior on user mutation paths
Focus on Repository-grounded verification of all user cache readers and writers
Focus on Authorization/security impact of stale cached user state
Focus on Error-handling, atomicity, and fallback behavior when database and cache operations diverge
Focus on TTL/expiration semantics and performance-consistency tradeoffs
checklist: Only report a stale-cache bug if you can cite at least one concrete cache read path in the current repository that consumes the same user cache key after updates; name the exact files/functions actually found.
Before referencing a cache key pattern such as `user:${userId}`, verify the exact key construction in code and quote the matching implementation; do not assume naming conventions.
Validate the precise changed function and line range from the diff before citing a location; do not emit line numbers or files that are not present in the patch/repository snapshot.

Inspect every concrete user mutation path in scope (e.g. update/profile/auth/account-state changes) and determine whether each path invalidates, refreshes, or intentionally bypasses cache; distinguish confirmed gaps from unreviewed areas.

Inspect every concrete user read path in scope and classify whether stale cached user data could affect only display fields or also authorization, identity, account status, roles, or security decisions.

If suggesting `cache.del` or write-through `cache.set`, evaluate and state the consistency model tradeoff: read-after-write freshness, failure modes, TTL interaction, and whether cache errors should fail the request or be best-effort.
Do not recommend write-through as equivalent to invalidation unless you verify serialization shape, TTL preservation, and that all readers can consume the written value unchanged.
Check whether cache entries have TTLs, versioning, or background refresh behavior; include these facts in the reasoning because they affect severity and user-visible staleness windows.
Review error handling around DB success + cache failure and DB failure + cache mutation; explicitly state whether partial failure can leave the system inconsistent and what behavior is acceptable.

Require regression tests to cover: pre-seeded stale cache followed by update and read; update when cache is absent; cache-operation failure behavior; and, if authorization/account fields are cached, a test proving fresh state is used for auth-sensitive reads.
If no security, error-handling, TTL, or performance issue is found, explicitly say those areas were checked and no grounded issue was identified; do not leave scope ambiguous.

Do not mention files such as `src/routes/profile.ts`, `src/routes/users.ts`, `src/routes/auth.ts`, or `src/middleware/auth.ts` unless you verified they exist and actually participate in the affected read/write flow.
test_templates: Verify the identified issue with a test case
Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

skill_name: Grounded Evidence & Diff Validation Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Evidence quality and grounding of every finding, Hallucinated files, symbols, and line references, Completeness across issue categories mentioned by evaluator feedback
risk_profile: Hallucinated repository structure
Invalid line/file references
Overstated impact without evidence
Incomplete category coverage
Weak fixes that ignore operational semantics
Insufficiently specific regression tests
context_plan: Focus on Evidence quality and grounding of every finding
Focus on Hallucinated files, symbols, and line references
Focus on Completeness across issue categories mentioned by evaluator feedback
Focus on Precision of fixes and observable test expectations
checklist: For every finding, provide a chain of evidence: changed code location, matching reader/writer counterpart, and concrete impact path. If any link is missing, do not raise the issue.
Never cite a file, function, route, middleware, or line number unless it was directly observed in the provided diff/repository snapshot.

If the review identifies only one issue, explicitly document which additional categories were checked—security, error handling, TTL/consistency semantics, and performance—and summarize the grounded result for each.
Reject generic fixes. Any proposed fix must specify ordering relative to DB write success, expected behavior on cache failure, and whether TTL/metadata must be preserved.
Require test suggestions to assert observable behavior, not just implementation details; include exact setup, action, and expected postconditions.
When severity is assigned, justify it using confirmed downstream impact (e.g. auth decision, user-visible stale profile, bounded TTL window) rather than hypothetical worst case.
If repository evidence is insufficient to prove broader impact, narrow the claim accordingly instead of extrapolating.
Flag missing information explicitly when needed: e.g. `TTL not visible in reviewed code, so staleness duration is unconfirmed.`
Do not allow invalid line references: line numbers must correspond to the reviewed snapshot/diff hunk, otherwise omit them.
Ensure the final review distinguishes confirmed bug, plausible risk needing verification, and no-issue-identified areas.
test_templates: Verify the identified issue with a test case
Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

risk_profile: Swallowed async processing errors
Changed API contract/semantics
False-success HTTP responses
Untracked background job failure

Insufficient observability
Concurrency/resource pressure
Behavior change without contract update

Focus on Promise lifecycle and unhandled/swallowed failures
Focus on HTTP response semantics for background vs awaited work
Focus on Operational robustness of long-running import tasks
Focus on Observability and failure reporting for import jobs
checklist: Verify whether removing `await` changes the endpoint from synchronous import completion to fire-and-forget behavior.
Check if `try/catch` still captures failures from `processLargeCSV`; flag that `.catch(...)` on the spawned promise prevents request-level error propagation.
Review whether returning HTTP 200 immediately is correct if import processing can later fail.
Check for swallowed errors: logging with `console.error` may hide operational failure from callers, monitoring, retries, or persistence.
Assess whether `processLargeCSV` should be delegated to a job queue or tracked task instead of detached in-request execution.
Check if the route now needs explicit job status/result reporting rather than `sendStatus(200)`.
Verify logging quality: ensure errors include enough context and are routed through the project's logging/monitoring conventions, not bare `console.error`.
Consider concurrency and backpressure risks if multiple `/import` requests now launch parallel background imports.
Confirm whether background execution is safe in this runtime (process shutdown, request cancellation, duplicate submissions, resource exhaustion).
Request tests or documentation updates if endpoint behavior intentionally changed from blocking import to asynchronous kickoff.
Recommend tests covering both immediate success response and downstream import failure behavior.

skill_name: Express Route Security, API Contract, and Serialization Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Only evidenced route behavior in the changed file(s) and shown context, Authentication/authorization enforcement as a conditional policy question, Response serialization safety for posts and nested author objects
risk_profile: Conditional accidental public exposure depending on router mount policy
Sensitive data leakage through serialized ORM entities or nested author objects
Schema drift from returning raw models
Async error-handling mismatch across Express versions or wrapper conventions
context_plan: Focus on Only evidenced route behavior in the changed file(s) and shown context
Focus on Authentication/authorization enforcement as a conditional policy question
Focus on Response serialization safety for posts and nested author objects
Focus on Express async error propagation patterns with framework/version awareness
Focus on Response-schema consistency and explicit field whitelisting
checklist: Do not claim a route is publicly exposed, mounted, unreachable, or missing middleware unless the diff or provided context shows the router registration/bootstrap path. If mount context is absent, phrase the finding conditionally: 'If this router is mounted without auth...' and request verification.
Do not cite files, imports, wrappers, or middleware that are not present in the diff/context. No hallucinated bootstrap files, helpers, or route registrations.
Validate every line reference against the shown snippet before reporting. If an issue spans the handler, reference the first relevant line of the shown block only.

Treat auth/authorization as a policy-sensitive check: report it only as a conditional risk unless the repository context explicitly states `/posts` must be protected or shows inconsistent protection relative to neighboring routes.

Inspect `res.json(...)` payloads for raw model/entity leakage. Require explicit allowlisting for nested `author` fields and also check whether raw `post` entities themselves may expose internal fields; recommend DTO shaping rather than mutating and returning ORM objects.

When recommending fixes for serialization, require a stable response contract: return newly constructed plain objects, define behavior for missing authors, and include regression tests for both nested author fields and top-level post field exposure.

For async error handling, do not state failure as certain unless the codebase context shows Express 4-style unwrapped async handlers. If framework/version/wrapper context is missing, phrase as compatibility risk and ask reviewers to verify the project's standard async error pattern.

Require tests to be grounded and contract-focused: unauthorized access tests only when policy is known; otherwise request either a public-access test plus field-safety assertions or protected-route tests once policy is confirmed.
test_templates: Verify the identified issue with a test case
Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

skill_name: Database Query Efficiency, Pagination, and DTO Mapping Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Evidence-based query behavior in the route body, N+1 query detection and batching/eager-loading opportunities, Bounded result sets, pagination, and parameter validation
risk_profile: N+1 database amplification
High latency from sequential dependent async operations when present
Memory and response-time growth from unbounded result sets
Incorrect serialization from mutating ORM/model instances instead of mapping DTOs
context_plan: Focus on Evidence-based query behavior in the route body
Focus on N+1 query detection and batching/eager-loading opportunities
Focus on Bounded result sets, pagination, and parameter validation
Focus on Concurrency/latency characteristics of async loops
Focus on Safe transformation of ORM/model instances into response DTOs
checklist: Only report N+1 when the code explicitly performs per-record dependent lookups, such as `findAll()` followed by `findById()` inside a loop/map. Quote that pattern directly in the finding.

Separate concerns precisely: one finding for unbounded `findAll()`/missing pagination, another for per-post author lookup amplification. Cross-reference them, but do not collapse them into a single vague performance complaint.

Check whether awaits inside loops are sequential or parallel. If the code uses `for ... of` with `await`, call out latency from serialization; if it uses `Promise.all`, do not misreport sequential behavior.
Recommend fixes that preserve response safety: prefer a single joined/eager-loaded paginated query or batched author fetch with deduplication plus explicit author field allowlisting.

Do not assume helper APIs such as `findByIds`, eager loading, or pagination signatures exist unless visible in the codebase/context. When proposing them, label them as examples and note that the exact API should match the project's data layer.

Inspect whether mutating returned `post` objects is safe. Prefer constructing plain response objects via DTO mapping or `toJSON()`/serialization helpers rather than attaching fields onto ORM instances.
Require bounded pagination guidance with concrete validation expectations: clamp or reject invalid `limit`/`offset`, define defaults, and specify a maximum page size.

Add test expectations that are measurable: verify page-size bounds, invalid query handling, deduplicated author fetches for repeated `authorId`s, and that fetching N posts does not trigger 1+N DB calls when instrumentation/mocks are available.
If the route returns nested related data, require tests confirming the optimized path still preserves the documented response schema and missing-author behavior.
Do not speculate about memory exhaustion, streaming, or table size beyond what unbounded fetch logically implies; keep severity and wording proportional to the evidenced query pattern.
test_templates: Verify the identified issue with a test case
Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

skill_name: Cache Mutation & Surrounding-Code Auditor (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Cache key consistency across read/write/delete paths in the touched module, Mutation-path correctness including invalidation, refresh, stale-read windows, and adjacent sibling functions, Completeness of review within directly changed or referenced surrounding code
risk_profile: stale cache entries after mutation
cache key mismatch causing ineffective invalidation
read/write path inconsistency in cache layer
weak fix scope leaving sibling buggy call sites
insufficient regression coverage
hallucinated breadth beyond inspected code
context_plan: Focus on Cache key consistency across read/write/delete paths in the touched module
Focus on Mutation-path correctness including invalidation, refresh, stale-read windows, and adjacent sibling functions
Focus on Completeness of review within directly changed or referenced surrounding code
Focus on Fix and regression-test sufficiency for all affected cache call sites
checklist: Start from the diff and enumerate every cache key literal, cache-key helper, and cache operation (`get`/`set`/`del`/`invalidate`) in the changed file before writing findings.

For each finding, require at least two grounded code references: one showing where data is read/populated and one showing where it is invalidated, deleted, or updated. Do not report a cache-key mismatch unless both sides are confirmed in inspected code.

After identifying a primary issue, inspect the rest of the touched file and any directly imported cache/helper modules for the same pattern in sibling functions (`get*`, `set*`, `delete*`, `update*`, preload helpers, batch loaders). Explicitly state whether additional inconsistencies were found or not found.
Do not cite files or abstractions that were not opened or are not present in the diff/context. If broader impact is uncertain, label it as a follow-up check rather than a confirmed bug.
Validate exact line references against current file contents; do not provide approximate line numbers.

Check for cache namespace inconsistencies in the touched area, including singular/plural drift, renamed helpers, and duplicated inline key construction. If duplicated construction exists, require centralizing key generation in a shared helper and name every touched-module call site that should adopt it.

Trace the full mutation path in the changed module: database write, cache invalidation/update, returned value, and subsequent read behavior. Explicitly assess stale-read risk and whether delete vs rewrite is correct; mention race/error-handling concerns only if visible in inspected code.
Explicitly distinguish confirmed defects, checked-but-safe areas, and unverified areas. If only one grounded issue exists after adjacent review, say so rather than speculating.

Require regression tests that verify behavior, not just helper invocation: warm cache with stale data, perform mutation, assert next read returns fresh DB state, and verify read/write/invalidate paths resolve to the same cache key. Include at least one negative/regression scenario and one end-to-end behavioral scenario.

Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

trigger: Use when reviewing Observed async failure paths in `src/services/email.ts` only, Grounded handling of repository lookup results and provider send failures, Service-level error semantics without inventing unsupported domain types
risk_profile: Unhandled async exceptions from external service calls
Potential null dereference from repository lookup result
Ambiguous failure semantics for downstream callers
Operational instability from nondeterministic dependency behavior
Reduced maintainability from hard-coded dependencies
Contract mismatch between service and db layer
Over-prescriptive fixes that are not grounded in visible service contracts
context_plan: Focus on Observed async failure paths in `src/services/email.ts` only
Focus on Grounded handling of repository lookup results and provider send failures
Focus on Service-level error semantics without inventing unsupported domain types
Focus on Deterministic behavior and testability of external email delivery integration
Focus on Validation only when justified by the visible function contract or surrounding code
checklist: Anchor every finding to code that is explicitly present in the diff or referenced failure summary; do not infer APIs, files, types, or behaviors not evidenced by the snippet/context.
Do not claim `db.users.findById` throws, returns null, or guarantees an `email` field unless the code or provided context supports that; instead phrase risks conditionally.
Verify whether `sendWelcomeEmail` dereferences `user.email` before checking that `user` exists; if so, report a concrete null/undefined dereference risk tied to that exact code.
Verify whether provider errors are rethrown raw or wrapped; if recommending wrapping, preserve original cause/context rather than replacing everything with a generic `Error` without rationale.
Flag nondeterminism only if the production path actually uses `Math.random()` or equivalent randomness; explicitly distinguish production behavior from test-only stubs.

Assess whether the email client is module-scoped and hard-coded; if yes, recommend an injectable parameter or equivalent seam strictly as a maintainability/testability improvement grounded in the code.
Require suggested fixes to preserve determinism in tests by injecting or mocking the email client only if the client is currently hard-coded or otherwise difficult to control in tests.
When suggesting input validation for `userId`, mark it as optional unless the visible code shows this layer owns boundary validation; do not present it as a mandatory defect without evidence.

Check that suggested tests map directly to observed branches in the code: success path, missing user before dereference, invalid/missing email before send, provider rejection after successful lookup, and negative assertions that the provider is not called when prerequisites fail.
Do not reference any file other than `src/services/email.ts` unless it is explicitly present in the review context, and do not cite line numbers unless they correspond to the provided snippet/diff.
Ensure every recommendation distinguishes confirmed defects from contingent risks, especially around repository return shape, validation ownership, and architectural expectations.

skill_name: Privacy-Safe Error Exposure Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing PII exposure in error messages and logs around email addresses, Privacy implications of propagated provider failures, Regression tests for privacy-safe failures in `src/services/email.ts`
risk_profile: PII leakage through exception messages or logs
Sensitive provider failure details exposed to callers
Hallucinated security/privacy requirements without code evidence
context_plan: Focus on PII exposure in error messages and logs around email addresses
Focus on Privacy implications of propagated provider failures
Focus on Regression tests for privacy-safe failures in `src/services/email.ts`
checklist: Review only privacy concerns that can be grounded in `src/services/email.ts` and the provided findings; do not hallucinate broader project patterns or nonexistent modules.

Check whether suggested error messages include full recipient email addresses; if so, assess privacy/PII leakage risk and prefer userId or redacted email unless explicit logging/error conventions are shown.
Do not recommend logging sensitive data or adding logs unless the code context shows an established logging mechanism and sanitization pattern.

Do not invent security issues unrelated to the visible code; only raise privacy/security findings directly tied to exposed email data, error propagation, or externally observable behavior in this service.
If the proposed fix includes contextual error messages, require at least one test expectation ensuring thrown messages do not expose full email addresses unless intentionally specified.
Check for incomplete fixes that wrap errors but discard original causes; if the language/runtime supports `cause`, recommend preserving it while still avoiding unnecessary PII exposure.
Ensure every recommendation distinguishes confirmed defects from contingent risks and avoids overstating privacy requirements not evidenced by the codebase context.
test_templates: Verify the identified issue with a test case
Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

skill_name: Grounded Express Auth & Integration Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Auth route implementation proven directly from diff, Router registration and middleware ordering verified in actual entrypoint files, JWT secret configuration and startup validation
risk_profile: Authentication bypass
Weak secret management
Sensitive data exposure
Unhandled async exceptions only where framework setup proves risk
Route registration regressions
Incorrectly grounded security findings
Invalid line/file references
Overstated integration assumptions
Hallucinated framework-dependent defects
Weak or non-minimal fixes
Insufficiently grounded test plans
Incorrect assumptions about middleware/body parsing
Version-specific Express behavior misreview
context_plan: Focus on Auth route implementation proven directly from diff
Focus on Router registration and middleware ordering verified in actual entrypoint files
Focus on JWT secret configuration and startup validation
Focus on Request parsing and input-shape safety only where body parsing is evidenced or absent
Focus on JWT payload compatibility with existing auth middleware based on inspected contract
checklist: Do not claim a bug unless you can cite the exact file and line(s) in the changed code or directly inspected surrounding code that proves it.
For every finding, state the evidence source: changed file, inspected dependency file, or repository convention. If none exists, do not present it as a confirmed issue.

Before flagging router-mount issues, verify the actual application entrypoint(s) and name the exact mount path and middleware order present or missing. If the entrypoint is not in scope, explicitly state 'unable to verify route registration from provided code' instead of asserting it.

Before flagging JWT claim mismatch, inspect the real auth middleware/req.user contract and quote the exact expected payload fields. If no such contract is visible, downgrade to a compatibility question, not a defect.
Do not prescribe JWT claims unless the authorization layer demonstrably consumes them. Prefer 'align token payload with middleware contract' only after inspecting that contract.

Before flagging async error-handling defects, verify the framework/version or existing async-wrapper/error-middleware pattern. Only claim unhandled async errors when repository evidence shows thrown promise rejections are not forwarded.

Treat Express async-handler behavior as version-sensitive. Require explicit evidence before saying try/catch is necessary; otherwise phrase as 'ensure current Express/error middleware handles rejected promises'.

Before flagging req.body destructuring/runtime-shape issues, verify whether JSON/body parsing middleware is registered. If parser presence is unknown, phrase the issue as conditional and explain the exact precondition required to reproduce it.

Do not assume req.body can be undefined if body-parsing middleware is visibly installed for the route path; only raise destructuring risk when parser absence, alternate content types, or non-object bodies are actually reachable from code.
When recommending JWT secret fixes, require a fail-fast configuration path and consistency between signing and verification secrets, but do not invent verification behavior unless inspected.
If suggesting startup validation, verify whether configuration is already centralized. Prefer improving the existing config path over duplicating checks inside routes.
Require fixes to be repository-compatible: reference the actual config/bootstrap module if one exists; otherwise propose a minimal local fix and clearly label broader refactors as optional.
Every suggested fix must include the narrowest safe code change first, then optional architectural cleanup second.
When recommending tests, include only tests that can be derived from observed code paths. Mark integration tests as conditional on verified route mounting and middleware presence.
Every suggested test must name whether it is unit, route-level, or full integration, and must correspond to a proven failure mode.
Explicitly separate proven defects from inferred risks using labels such as 'Confirmed' and 'Needs verification'.
Do not reference files that were not provided or inspected; if a likely file exists but was not seen, ask for it or note the limitation.
Never cite a line number unless it maps to the reviewed file content; otherwise cite the symbol/function name.
Add a 'grounding note' whenever a finding depends on unseen files, framework version, or deployment configuration.
test_templates: Verify the identified issue with a test case
Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

skill_name: Express Route Grounding & Integration Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Confirmed route behavior visible in the diff, Conditional risks requiring repository context, Express version-sensitive async error handling
risk_profile: Grounding failures from missing repository context
Version-specific Express behavior misclassification
False positives about route exposure or mounting
context_plan: Focus on Confirmed route behavior visible in the diff
Focus on Conditional risks requiring repository context
Focus on Express version-sensitive async error handling
Focus on Router export and mounting evidence
Focus on Access-control verification grounded in shown code
checklist: Only label an issue as a defect when the changed code itself proves it. If auth, export, mounting, or framework-version details are not shown, phrase them as conditional risks and explicitly state what evidence is missing.

Do not claim a route is unprotected unless the diff or surrounding file shows the absence of auth middleware where project conventions require it. If the endpoint may be intentionally public, say so and limit the finding to 'verify intended access control'.

Do not claim async error handling is broken unless the Express version or project wrapper pattern makes that true. If version is unknown, state: 'On Express 4 this needs try/catch/next; on Express 5 native async forwarding may already handle this.'

Do not claim the router is not exported or not mounted unless the file end or registration entrypoint is visible and confirms that. If export/mounting is not shown, ask for verification instead of filing a definite bug.

Never reference files, modules, middleware names, or helper APIs that are not present in the diff unless clearly marked as examples. Avoid hallucinated project paths or invented repository methods as if they already exist.
When suggesting fixes, separate 'minimal safe fix from shown code' from 'optional improvement if project supports X'.
Require line references to map to code actually implicated by the issue.
For every non-confirmed concern, include a verification step and the exact evidence needed to upgrade it to a confirmed defect.
test_templates: Verify the identified issue with a test case
Test edge cases and boundary conditions
Ensure regression tests prevent reintroduction

skill_name: API Response Safety & Contract Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing Response payload safety, Nested author object shaping, Schema and contract changes
risk_profile: Sensitive data leakage via nested author objects
Breaking API response contract
Unsafe mutation of persistence-layer objects
context_plan: Focus on Response payload safety
Focus on Nested author object shaping
Focus on Schema and contract changes
Focus on Unsafe mutation of persistence-layer entities
Focus on Public-safe serialization tests
checklist: Treat attaching raw `db.users.findById(...)` results to response objects as a confirmed data-exposure risk when the code returns the full user record without projection.
Require fixes to project nested author data through an explicit allowlist of fields and to serialize plain response objects rather than mutating persistence-layer entities in place.

Call out API contract changes explicitly: adding `author` to each post changes response shape and may require versioning, client coordination, or contract tests. Phrase this as confirmed if the diff enriches the response.
Do not assume which fields are sensitive unless examples are framed as possibilities. State that any fields returned by the user repository beyond the intended public subset may leak.
Suggested tests must assert both positive and negative expectations: approved author fields are present, unapproved fields are absent, and the exact response shape is stable.
If recommending removal of nested author data, explain that this is an alternative product/API decision, not the only correctness fix.
Ensure line references point to the enrichment/serialization lines, not unrelated route declarations.
If mutation semantics of returned records are uncertain, prefer mapping to plain response objects over in-place entity mutation.

skill_name: Data Access Performance & Related-Record Reliability Reviewer (Learned)
trigger: Use when reviewing N+1 query patterns proven by the code, Batch loading alternatives without inventing APIs, Missing-author behavior
risk_profile: Latency spikes from serial lookups
Inefficient database access under load
Ambiguous behavior for missing related records
context_plan: Focus on N+1 query patterns proven by the code
Focus on Batch loading alternatives without inventing APIs
Focus on Missing-author behavior
Focus on Performance-oriented test design
checklist: Flag per-post author lookup inside a loop as a confirmed N+1 issue only when the code visibly performs one user fetch per post.

Do not prescribe a specific repository method such as `findByIds` as if it exists. Recommend batching, eager loading, or joining generically; if giving sample code, label missing APIs as illustrative.
Include correctness expectations for missing authors: ask whether the intended contract is `author: null`, omission, or error, and tests should lock that behavior down.
Performance tests should verify bounded database-call counts or equivalent batching behavior, not just generic latency claims.
When discussing reliability, distinguish confirmed performance defects from conditional operational risks; do not overstate scalability conclusions beyond the visible serial query pattern.
Require line references to point to the loop and lookup responsible for the repeated queries.
